content: We can frame LLM self-reflection mechanisms as an iterative linguistic equivalent of optical light reflexion: just as physical reflexion bounces light off a surface to reverse its direction and create a visible image of the original object, LLM self-reflection bounces the model’s own output back against its problem-solving perspective to reverse its initial reasoning direction and generate a clearer, corrected representation of the solution. The language learning definition of reflection as a deliberate review of one’s own output further reinforces that this parallel is not just metaphorical, but functional: both educational linguistic reflection and technical LLM self-reflection rely on redirecting attention back to one’s prior work to improve future outputs.
